params.text:
How substances get into the body

To cause harm, a hazardous substance usually has to get into or onto the body. Understanding these routes of entry helps you see why a particular control – such as gloves, a mask or good ventilation – is used. Blocking the route of entry is how we prevent exposure.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: The main routes by which hazardous substances enter the body.

dialogs.answer: Breathing in dusts, fumes, vapours, mists or aerosols. Controlled by ventilation, avoiding dry sweeping and, where needed, respiratory protection.
Substances soaking through the skin or splashing into the eyes. Controlled by gloves, aprons, eye protection and good hand care.
Swallowing a substance, often by hand-to-mouth contact, eating or smoking without washing hands. Controlled by hand washing and not eating in work areas.
A substance entering through broken skin, for example a needlestick from a sharp. Controlled by safe sharps handling and prompt disposal in a sharps bin.
